title: Define and implement an interface
instruction:
An interface defines method signatures — no body (unless using `default`). Classes `implement` the interface and provide the bodies.

Run this — both `Dog` and `Robot` implement `Speakable` differently.
successMessage: makeSpeak accepts ANY Speakable — Dog, Robot, or anything else that implements the interface. The interface is the contract; classes decide the implementation.

title: Multiple interfaces
instruction:
A class can implement multiple interfaces — separated by commas:

```java
class Duck implements Swimmable, Flyable, Speakable { ... }
```

This is how Java gets around single inheritance limitation.
successMessage: Duck implements both interfaces. A Swimmable variable can hold a Duck, a Flyable variable can also hold a Duck — same object, different 'views' via different interface types.

title: default methods — add to interfaces without breaking
instruction:
`default` methods in interfaces have a body — they're optional to override.

This lets you add new methods to an interface without breaking all existing implementations.
successMessage: Invoice only implemented format() — it got print() and printLoud() for free. This is how Java's collections were updated (Iterable.forEach, etc.) without breaking all existing code.

title: Comparable — natural ordering
successMessage: Sorted by grade ascending — Bob first (72), Carol last (95). Comparable makes your class work with all of Java's sorting infrastructure for free.

title: Functional interfaces and lambdas
successMessage: Lambdas implement functional interfaces. MathOp is the interface; (a, b) -> a + b is the implementation. You'll use this pattern constantly with Java's streams in Chapter 17.

title: Build a payment system
instruction:
Final challenge — no starter code.

Define:
- `interface PaymentMethod` with `boolean processPayment(double amount)` and `String getType()`
- `class CreditCard implements PaymentMethod` — always succeeds if amount <= 5000
- `class PayPal implements PaymentMethod` — always succeeds if amount <= 2000  
- `class Bitcoin implements PaymentMethod` — always succeeds (no limit)

Write `checkout(double amount, PaymentMethod... methods)` that tries each method in order and prints which one succeeded.
hint: For each method, if processPayment(amount) returns true, print the type and return.

codeChecks.message: At least one class implements PaymentMethod.
Define checkout method with PaymentMethod... params.

successMessage: A polymorphic payment system — checkout doesn't care which payment method it gets. Add a new payment type? Just implement PaymentMethod. No existing code changes. Chapter 12 done!
